titleEn: As If I Never Confided My Secret

verses.sadr: أعياش ارع أو لا ترع حقي
verses.ajuz: وصل أو لا تصل أبدا وسيلي

translationEn: It is as if I never confided my innermost secret to you two, and you never witnessed my burning passion amid my bewilderment. I left my eye to smolder and bleed, weeping both in rightful grief and in vain excess. I am wholly devoted, for my solace has given itself over to weeping and wailing. In Alexandria there is the ruin of a house that has faded — and with it my patience and my resolve have faded too. Within it I recalled my forgotten consolations, while the fire of my longing blazed anew. Ever it renews grief and yearning for it, wearing upon it the worn traces of ancient ruins. I lost you in a time that stripped away every loss, and its calamities devoured every monster of fate. Its disasters erased the paths of meaning and its darkness extinguished the lamps of reason. The cleverness of the intelligent man cannot reach its wonders, nor can the soundest thought grasp them. Even were al-Khalil resurrected, its calamities would overwhelm even the wits of al-Khalil himself! O 'Ayyash — honor my right or do not, maintain ties with me or never maintain them at all. I see that the one who showed you error as guidance will soon clothe you in the garment of rumor and gossip. Epic poems wrought from the finest substance of poetry will make you forget your father's reading and your grandfather's books. Is one like you to be hoped for — were it not for my distant circumstances and my entanglement in my narrow affairs? Your delusion about the delayed hope that eludes is matched by your certainty of the quick despair that attains. Hope has settled in the courtyards of my heart the way miserliness settles in the heart of a miser. An opinion that shook good faith until its water flowed through all my breadth and length. So my standing at the door of your generosity benefited me as much as the lovesick man who stands at a crumbling ruin. My wishes encamped within my breast the way a lingering glance rests on a smooth, gentle cheek. I was once more proud than one content with compensation from an indifferent forgiver of a fool. Now I have become more abased than a subtle meaning that needs a great mind to grasp it. I do not know whether my blindness in seeking has ruined me, or your blindness to what is right. When can fruit be good and branches flourish if the roots are corrupt? I summoned you to generosity — but you are worthless. I wronged you: you are not among the people of generosity! Both your parents are from Yemen — but both parents of your giving are from the base tribe of Sulul! Go slowly — for your ignorance will soon unveil for you a long darkness of disgrace. And diminish your scheming, for when you are scorched by my fire, your scheming is less than nothing. The bitternesses of staying near you fade away and vanish in the sweetness of departure. I shall travel, knowing that nothing cures my sickness like swift travel and a brisk pace. Even if your right hand were a thousand seas, each one overflowing with a thousand Niles.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: my innermost secret, what is hidden within me
my passionate love, burning attachment
my bewilderment, my forgetting caused by distress
ruins of encampments, traces of the beloved's abode — a central image of the nasib
the clever, intelligent man
a swift pace of the camel — used metaphorically for cure through travel
a brisk, easy trot — a comfortable swift gait
the smooth, gently sloping cheek — a classical beauty descriptor

context: A long wafir qasida blending the ghazal opening with a sharp hija (invective) against a man named 'Ayyash. Abu Tammam laments a ruined dwelling in Alexandria, then turns his grief into biting satire against a patron who failed him. The poem moves from tender longing to scathing denunciation, culminating in an announcement of departure.

titleEn: I Heard Abdullah Has Begun to Wail

verses.ajuz: في نتف شعر الخد حين يسنبل !

translationEn: I was told that Abdullah has begun to wail — indeed, time shifts its fortunes among its people! When the wretch began to apply hair dye, he shed a tear — for young gazelles, the first growth of fur is the beginning. He plucks out hairs to restore his beauty after its fading, not knowing that beauty cannot be manufactured. He plucks the soft first beard — what is his excuse for plucking cheek hair once it has grown into a full beard?

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: to wail, cry out in lamentation
to apply dye (tar or pitch) — here used for dyeing grey hair
young gazelles — whose first fur grows at a tender age; a metaphor here
the growing of a beard, from لحية (beard)
the side teeth, or the first hairs growing on the sides of the face
to grow like an ear of grain — to grow long and thick, of a beard

context: A short satirical poem mocking a man named Abdullah who futilely tries to conceal his age by plucking grey beard hairs and dyeing his hair. Abu Tammam uses sharp irony: the attempt to restore lost youth only exposes its absence more fully.

titleEn: Your Love of Older Men Is Telling

verses.sadr: ثكلتك يا أخي إن كنت عندي

translationEn: Your infatuation with older men tells me that the millstone has been turned upside down. Otherwise, younger ones are sweeter in company and more desirable if you sought to act with them. When did one of your kind ever desire to encounter grown men? May you be bereaved, my brother — if in my view your affair is sound, you would be better off chewing on mules!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the millstone — a symbol of natural order; turning it upside down means perverting nature
the lower, stationary millstone — to flip the raha on its thafal means total disorder
one who practices the act of Lot's people — a sodomite, used as a slur here
may you be bereaved — a strong curse invoking the loss of a child or loved one
to chew, to gnaw — used here in a figurative insult

context: A four-verse satirical poem of personal invective, typical of Abu Tammam's hija genre. The poem mocks the subject's sexual proclivities with crude humor, characteristic of the more unrestrained poems in the Diwan.

titleAr: هل الله لو أشركت كان معذبي
titleEn: Would God Punish Me More Than This

verses.sadr: هل الله لو أشركت كان معذبي
هلموا اعجبوا من أنبه الناس كلهم

translationEn: Would God, if I had committed polytheism, punish me with more than this — that I pin my hope on your standing? Come, marvel at the man most renowned among all people whose means to what he seeks is the basest of intermediaries. Should a man be content with weakness in his means of access when his every movement is itself a means of access?

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: to commit shirk — polytheism, associating partners with God; the gravest sin in Islam
your rank, your prestige, your standing in society
most renowned, most distinguished
means of access, pretext, intermediary
obscure, base, ignoble — the opposite of نبيه
means of access, intermediaries, channels of petition

context: A three-verse epigram that turns on a striking theological conceit: to rely on the poet's intercessory standing is punishment worse than the punishment for polytheism. Abu Tammam uses this hyperbole to flatter an unnamed patron while complaining of his own desperate position.

titleAr: ستعلم يا عياش إن كنت تعلم
titleEn: You Will Know, O 'Ayyash

verses.sadr: ستعلم يا عياش إن كنت تعلم
أبى لك أن تأبى المخازي كلها

verses.ajuz: لديك الغنى أو ليس في الأرض درهم

verses.sadr: فلما بدا لي منك لؤم يحفه
تركتك ما إن في أديمك ظاهر
verses.ajuz: ولا باطن إلا ولي فيه ميسم

translationEn: You will know, O 'Ayyash, if you are capable of knowing, and you will regret — if your ignorance leaves you — you will regret. What prevented you from refusing all shameful acts was a father of Andrahali stock and a grandfather who was a teacher of disgrace. I suspended my judgment of you to the point that I imagined you possessed great wealth, or that there was not a single dirham left on earth. I held back blame from you to the point that it seemed as if glory had given you its protection, or as if I were struck dumb. Then when your baseness appeared to me, surrounded by a provincialism that breeds in it the habit of strutting arrogance, I let you go — there is not an outer surface nor an inner depth in you that does not bear my brand. To ask you for anything is easier than to be struck blind and dumb, and sweeter than your beneficence is pus and blood.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: shameful acts, disgraceful deeds
to hold back, restrain — used of restraining tears or blame
baseness, meanness, contemptibility of character
provincial coarseness, the rough manner of bedouin or village people
strutting arrogance, affected pride of the ill-bred who pretend to refinement
a branding iron; brand — the poet has marked him permanently with satire
pus — the ultimate expression of disgust with the patron's generosity

context: A seven-verse invective qasida against 'Ayyash, a figure who appears repeatedly in Abu Tammam's hija poems. The poem follows the classical hija structure: withholding of blame followed by a devastating release. The final verse — comparing the patron's gifts to pus and blood — is among the most scathing lines in classical Arabic satire.

titleEn: He Swore Truly by His Loaf of Bread

verses.ajuz: ' لا والرغيف ' فذاك البر من قسمه !

verses.sadr: قد كان يعجبني لو أن غيرته

translationEn: He speaks truly in his oath if he earnestly swears: 'No, by the loaf of bread!' — for that bread is the piety of his portion! And if you intend to confront him, seize him by his loaf — for it reaches his flesh and blood more than anything else! I used to admire him — I only wish his jealousy over his flatbreads were directed toward his womenfolk instead!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: his oath, what he swears by
a round loaf of bread — the subject's most treasured possession, used as a mock sacred object
piety, righteousness — here ironic: his only piety is his bread
his loaf, his specific piece of bread
his large round flatbreads — jurdaqa is a Persian loanword for a large flat loaf
his womenfolk, his protected female kin — a loaded word implying failed masculine honor

context: A three-verse satirical epigram mocking a man whose obsessive attachment to bread is greater than his sense of honor. Abu Tammam inverts the classical values of virility and protectiveness to devastating comic effect: the man's only sacred object is his loaf of bread.

titleEn: The Zanj and Romans Surpass You

verses.sadr: لما بدا لي من صميمك ما بدا
verses.ajuz: بل لم يصب لك لا أصيب صميم

translationEn: The Zanj are more noble than you, and the Romans — even ill-fortune is more auspicious than you, and misfortune more blessed! O 'Ayyash, you are truly base — and since you became the object of my request, I too have become base. Ill-gotten gain is sweeter in prospect than your giving, and so are boiling water and pus and the zaqqum tree. An impure man whose affairs are managed by vile character traits — a stubborn one whose affairs are managed by blame. His dwellings have not a single courtyard but that in it there is a petitioner left empty-handed. Wretched courtyards never home to a master, in which no noble man has ever grazed. When what was revealed of your true nature was revealed — nay, your true nature was never rightly constituted, may mine not be struck! I sent forth against you in satire squadrons of poems, and the whole world turned against you while you sit still at home. They have affixed your lineage to the wild fig tree in humiliation, while the wormwood and artemisia laugh at you. The layers of fat on you — it is no secret that they were not built from eating the moringa or southernwood. O drinker of camel milk to console yourself — who possesses patience, and who possesses the cheese curds? The one who claims Sawran as his grandfather's home — tell me: who then owns Ahnaz and the Fayyum?

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the Zanj — East Africans, used here as a rhetorical foil: even they surpass you
ill-gotten, forbidden gain — sweeter than the target's generosity
the festering pus of the inhabitants of Hell — a Quranic term (69:36)
the bitter tree of Hell — Quranic (37:62), used to intensify the insult
the wild fig tree — a lowly, common tree, used to demean the subject's lineage
wormwood, a bitter desert plant — here personified as laughing at the target
a type of fresh cheese or curd — a humble, lowly food

context: A twelve-verse kamil hija qasida against 'Ayyash, one of several in this diwan. Abu Tammam deploys Quranic vocabulary (zaqqum, ghaslin) to heighten the insult, and references Egyptian toponyms (Ahnaz, the Fayyum) to mock the target's false claims of noble lineage. The poem's ferocity is characteristic of Abu Tammam's satirical register.

titleEn: Do You Know What Lightning Flash You Watch

verses.sadr: يرى في كل واد أنت فيه

translationEn: Do you know what lightning flash you watch, and toward what deadly place you incline with trust? How long, and how much does my forbearance shield you from my harm — a forbearance that remains gentle toward you even in my anger! It is as if you have not benefited from my sleeplessness, when drowsiness embraces the deep sleeper, and from my heart turning over, away from my tongue, when cares toss it all night. So you are not the base one — rather, the time in which you rose to prominence is the base one. Do you aspire to be counted the noble one of your people, when no noble man circulates near your door? Like one who made the lowest ground his resting place and yet claims the stars are his brothers. I swear by the day of Abu Sa'id's return — a happy day, truly a mighty one! A young man, among the most generous of young men in giving freely to those who seek him, with no creditor upon him. Sleep then, and let your honor sleep — but the poems are wrathful: they neither sleep nor let others sleep. They spend the night like a great serpent stirring against you in a rocky cleft, one whose bite-victim is never cured. They see in every valley you inhabit your baseness, wandering forever, roaming without end.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a flash of lightning — used figuratively for a fleeting, dangerous temptation or hope
to recline trustingly, to surrender oneself to — from نام, to sleep or incline
drowsiness, the first onset of sleep — lighter than نوم
the one who sleeps deeply and long, a heavy sleeper
the lowest point, the nadir — the opposite of the heights and stars
a large snake, a great serpent — the poems are compared to a deadly snake
one bitten by a snake — a euphemism (literally 'the healthy one') for the snakebite victim

context: A twelve-verse wafir hija addressed to the recurring target 'Ayyash, interspersed with praise of an unnamed patron Abu Sa'id. The poem's central metaphor — the sleepless, wrathful poem-serpents that roam every valley — is a brilliant image of satire's inexorable spread and longevity.

titleEn: Now You Have Let the Wolves Among the Sheep

verses.ajuz: وصرت أضيع من لحم على وضم

verses.sadr: قد كنت تحكي حطيطا صالحا فغدت
واجرت جودا بما قد كنت تمنعه
verses.ajuz: ما كل جود الفتى يدني من الكرم !

verses.sadr: إن أبل فيك بأن أصبحت منتهبا
verses.ajuz: فالمرء قد يبتلى في صالح الحرم

translationEn: Now you have let the wolves loose among the sheep, and I have become more lost than meat on a butcher's block. You used to resemble a proper Hutayt — but now your thighs are more eloquently written upon than your palms with a pen! I used to call you Abdullah before — now I have lost that and call you Zayd without any respect. You have been generous with what you used to withhold — but not every generosity of a young man brings him closer to true nobility! If I am being tested in you by the fact that you have become a plunderer, then a man is sometimes tested through those dear to him.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: wolves — the plural of ذئب, here for those who exploit the vulnerable
a butcher's block or chopping board — the helpless meat upon it is utterly exposed
Hutayt — a proper name used ironically for someone who seemed respectable
one who is plundered, robbed — or one who plunders
one's protected folk, one's womenfolk or loved ones — a trial through those one holds dear

context: A five-verse basit invective poem, likely against the same milieu of ungrateful or base patrons and acquaintances. The opening image of wolves let loose among sheep is vivid and proverb-like. The poem employs mockery of the subject's body and reputation alongside a resigned philosophical closing.

titleEn: Many a Coarse-Natured Man Excels
translationEn: Many a coarse-natured man is coarser in his very flesh and blood than my own tenderness. His beneficence is a gift that, when struck like a flint to give to a free man, turns him back from his own ambitions. Thus he preserved my face from his largesse and protected my honor from being diminished by his generosity! Praise be to God who delivered me from him, my skin intact, unscathed by his favors.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: coarse of nature, rough-tempered, lacking refinement
tenderness, delicacy of character — the opposite of coarseness
to strike fire, as with flint — here used for the act of giving generosity
gift, support, bestowal — from the root رفد meaning to assist
intact of skin, unharmed — a metaphor for escaping without obligation or humiliation

context: A four-verse manasrih poem reflecting on a patron from whom Abu Tammam is relieved to be free. The poem inverts the expected praise: the patron's coarseness is so great that it actually protects the poet's dignity by repelling him. Gratitude is addressed to God, not the patron.

titleEn: See How the Two New Ones Wear Us Away

verses.sadr: وامهد لنفسك من قبل الممات ولا

translationEn: Do you not see how the two new ones — day and night — wear us away, and how we play our lives out in secret and in the open? Do not lean upon this world and its glittering ornaments, for its dwelling places are no true home. Prepare a resting place for yourself before death comes, and let not the abundance of companions and brothers deceive you. If they were ever able to benefit a created being dear to them, they would have repelled death from the wife of Ma'dan!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the two new ones — day and night, which perpetually renew and thereby wear things away
to wear us out, to exhaust us — from بلى, to become worn, threadbare
its ornaments, its gilded adornments — the glittering but deceptive beauty of worldly life
to prepare, to smooth out a place — here for the afterlife
a proper name — a figure whose wife died despite his wealth and connections, a proverb of death's inevitability

context: A four-verse basit ascetic poem (zuhdiyya), unusually moralistic in tone for Abu Tammam's diwan. The poem uses the traditional motif of 'the two new ones' (day and night) as destroyers of life. The closing reference to Ma'dan's wife is a proverb illustrating that no wealth or companionship can ward off death.

titleEn: The Days Have Exposed You, O Man

verses.sadr: إن تكن قد فضضت بعدي فليست

translationEn: The days have exposed you, O man — may what you have humiliated never suffer humiliation! If you have been opened up after me — it is no novelty that pomegranates split! Hands unfolded you after your chastity — you were folded within modesty and kept safe. O you who preceded in indulging pleasures and revelry — where now is that burning ardor? Every trainer who challenged you, you would say: between you and me is the racetrack. Shall I be wretched through you while another is made happy by your love — then my love would indeed be that of a cuckold!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: exposed you, unveiled your true nature — the days as a revealer of character
to be opened, broken open — used here of a seal or enclosure being forced
pomegranate — its splitting when ripe is a metaphor here for moral rupture
revelry, feasting, unbridled pleasure — from قصف, to break or indulge
intense burning ardor, the heat of passion — also a stubborn horse who refuses to move
a horse-trainer, one who breaks in horses — a metaphor for a rival or challenger

context: A six-verse khafif poem mixing reproach with elements of both love poetry and invective. The poem addresses someone whose hidden nature has been exposed by time, possibly a former companion who has changed. The closing verse deploys the vulgar term 'cuckold' (kashkhan) as a self-deprecating joke about the poet's doomed attachment.

titleEn: A Racing Horse of Streaming Swift Strides

verses.sadr: حلقت إن لم تثبت أن حافره
verses.ajuz: من صخر تدمر أو من وجه عثمان !

translationEn: A swimmer of a horse — streaming with swift running like pouring rain — faithful in the race, never deceiving. Its ankle joints are dry and lean, yet its legs have not gone thirsty — so let your eyes rest on what is parched yet full! If you could see it straining forward while pebbles crack beneath its hooves, splitting in pairs and singly — you would swear its hooves were made of the rock of Tadmur or the face of 'Uthman!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: swimmer — applied to a horse moving through air like a swimmer through water
pouring, streaming — of rain or swift movement
swiftness, the act of racing — from عدا, to run fast
the ankle joints or fetlocks of a horse — should be lean and dry for speed
straining forward eagerly, bent intently on the course
the tips of the hooves — the hard forepart of a horse's hoof

context: A four-verse basit horse-description poem (wasf al-khayl), a genre beloved in Arabic literature. Abu Tammam describes a racing horse with characteristic paradoxes: dry joints yet vigorous legs. The final verse humorously insults 'Uthman by comparing the horse's hard hooves to his face.

titleEn: Ahmad Has Gone and Left Me Sorrowful

verses.sadr: ما لنور الربيع في غير حسن

translationEn: By God, Ahmad has gone away, and his absence has cut from my nights a piece of pure grief. I was left behind after him among people who clothed me in patience over the blows of fate. The blossoms of spring have no beauty in what is not beauty — and they have no share in the changing of colors. My soul disowned them — but that disowning is nothing but the extremity of my recognition of what true worth is. And the wrongs of the one who wrongs will one day remind you of the kindness of the one who is kind. The abundance of copper to the right and left has weakened the preciousness of pure gold!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the blows of fate, calamities — from حدث, an event, especially a misfortune
the blossoms of spring — a conventional image of beauty that here fails to console
recognition, deep knowledge — the paradox: disowning these people stems from knowing them too well
copper, brass — common and base metal, contrasted with gold
pure gold found naturally — the rarest and most precious metal

context: A six-verse khafif elegy for a companion named Ahmad who has departed or died. The poem uses the classical motif of comparing the absent noble friend to gold, and the unworthy replacements to copper. The final verse — abundance of copper diminishes the value of gold — is a memorable epigram.

titleEn: Know the Mother of Ibn al-A'mash

verses.sadr: لا تحسبن أني افتريت على التي

translationEn: Know the mother of Ibn al-A'mash — she is libertine, and how easy and available her favors are! A large-buttocked woman who, when a frightened man comes seeking her refuge in her crevice, does well to receive him charitably. If her lusts were to invest silver for profit or gold, she would be a mine. Do not think I have slandered the one who bore you — I have merely slandered fornication itself.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a libertine woman of easy virtue — used in classical Arabic as a term of insult
a woman with large buttocks — used here as an insulting physical descriptor
her sexual desire, her lust — from غلمة, ardent sexual appetite
to trade, to acquire provisions — here the lust 'trading' in silver and gold
a mine, a source of ore — she would be inexhaustibly rich if lust were currency

context: A four-verse satirical attack on the mother of Ibn al-A'mash, a recurring target in Abu Tammam's hija. The closing verse is a famous example of the classical technique of double insult — ostensibly defending the mother while actually redoubling the slander.

titleEn: Which Face Will You Show Me in Misr

verses.ajuz: سان أم وجه غير ذي إحسان ؟ !
نك في كل محضر أن تراني

verses.sadr: ولئن كنت غير ذاك فما أن
كل يوم آتيك في حاجة أب
ثم لم أحظ منك في حاجة قط

translationEn: I wonder — with which of your two faces will you meet me in Misr tomorrow when we come together? Will it be a face with the open brightness of the benefactor, or a face that knows no beneficence? For if you are a benefactor, it will delight you in every gathering to see me. And if you are otherwise, you will hold no authority over us tomorrow. Every day I come to you with a need — I humiliate both my face and my tongue in it together. Then I have gained from you nothing in any need but refusal and deprivation! You are a one-eyed deputy — by the right of the Prophet of God, O Salm, you are of 'Uthman's kind.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: openness, brightness, cheerfulness of face — the look of one who gives freely
refusal, rejection — the proud turning away of a request
deprivation, being left empty-handed
one-eyed — used here as an insult, implying deficiency and poor judgment
successor, deputy — here used with a derogatory connotation of unworthy replacement

context: A seven-verse khafif poem addressed to a petitioned official, asking which face he will show — the face of generosity or refusal. The closing verse attacks the official by comparing him unfavorably to 'Uthman and calling him one-eyed — a direct personal insult closing what began as a petition poem.

titleEn: Baghdad's Mourner Has Taken His Station

verses.sadr: كانت على ما بها والحرب موقدة
ترجى لها عودة في الدهر صالحة

translationEn: Truly Baghdad's mourning-crier has taken his station — so let its weepers weep for it, for the ruin wrought by time. It was in such a state, while war was kindled and fire extinguished beauty in its environs. One hoped for it a good return in the course of time — but now even hope within it has diminished, and its hopers have given up. It is like an old woman whose youth has passed, and from whom the completeness that once enriched her has departed. Then a co-wife was fastened to her — bright, radiant, like the sun — more beautiful than her in the eyes of any beholder.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: her mourning-crier, the one who announces the death — نعى means to announce a death
despair — from يئس (to despair), here personified as something that shrinks within Baghdad
her youth — used of Baghdad as an aged woman whose glory has faded
a co-wife — the rival city that has displaced Baghdad in beauty and favor
brilliant, radiant, shining white — the epithet of the rival city outshining Baghdad

context: A five-verse basit elegy for Baghdad, likely composed during or after the civil strife of the Abbasid period. Abu Tammam uses the extended metaphor of a discarded first wife for Baghdad — she who was once glorious has been supplanted by a shining rival city. This is one of the few poems in which Abu Tammam mourns a city rather than a person.

titleAr: لا ترث لابن الأعمش الكشخان من
titleEn: Do Not Pity Ibn al-A'mash the Cuckold

verses.sadr: لا ترث لابن الأعمش الكشخان من

translationEn: Do not pity Ibn al-A'mash the cuckold — for licentiousness and harlotry are cheaply available in his household. Look at the son of two fornicators — you will find both his horns wrestling in his eyes. His old woman blocked the road to Fayash and redirected the company of her visitors to him. My concern is not with him — my concern is with what rotten thing stands upon him.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the cuckold — from Persian/Turkish loanword, a man who tolerates his wife's infidelity
here: license, permission — used ironically for free access to licentiousness
prostitution, harlotry — from بغى, to transgress
two horns — the horns of the cuckold, a cross-cultural symbol of marital dishonor
wrestling together, grappling — the two horns of shame fighting in his eyes

context: A four-verse kamil invective against the recurring target Ibn al-A'mash. Abu Tammam employs the cuckold (kashkhan) insult with graphic directness. The poem represents some of the rawest hija in the classical Arabic tradition, preserved in the diwan without censorship.

titleEn: By Which Stars of Your Face Is Light Found
translationEn: By which stars of your face can light be found, O Abu al-Hasan, when your very nature is refusal? Will you leave my need a target for procrastination — and yet you are the bucket in it and the rope? Bring the family of Idris close to me through Badr — for the mediation of giving is itself the giving. Take them by charm and incantations — for swift camels are stirred to travel by the camel-driver's song. Either my poetry about them will work its magic, or your alchemy will work its wonders! And say to the man 'Uthman words so grand the open land is too narrow to contain them. Has the speech of a young man who prays, praising you with the finest praise, not moved you? So do what glory wills in it — for glory does what it wills. You are the man whom high stations adore, and in whose gifts hope holds full authority. You cannot delight in a day of praise for which you are renowned while your wealth is unharmed. And praise among peoples, if not broadcast by generous reward, is itself satire.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the stars of your face — the glowing features that should guide and illuminate
the rope of the well-bucket — the means of drawing up what is needed
alchemy — the transformative art; here used as a metaphor for the patron's transformative generosity
the camel-driver's song, huda — which urges camels to walk faster
high stations, lofty aspirations, the summit of noble qualities
satire, invective — here: unpaid praise becomes its opposite

context: An eleven-verse wafir madih addressed to Abu al-Hasan, asking him to intercede with the Alid family of Idris and with a man named 'Uthman. The poem's final verse — that uncompensated praise is satire — is one of Abu Tammam's most quoted epigrams, encapsulating the transactional economy of panegyric poetry.

titleEn: O Ornament of the World and Unifier of Its Ranks

verses.sadr: ويا شمس أرضيها التي تم نورها
ولي عدة قد راث عني نجاحها

verses.ajuz: ثكلت من الدنيا على حسن وائها

translationEn: O ornament of the world and unifier of its scattered members, through whose justice in it is the completion of its splendor! O sun of its earth, whose light is complete — so that the lands boast before the sun with the splendor he provides. Your gift does not exhaust itself, it engulfs all desires and keeps the faces of the seekers bright with its water. Glances were hurled at me from every side, as if I were suspect among them for the way they were aimed. I have a promise long delayed in its fulfillment — and your glory is the closest guide in claiming it. I complained — though complaint is not my custom — but the soul overflows when it is full. I have no intercessor but yourself — for I have been bereft by this world, beautiful as its companionship is.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: unifier of its scattered members — one who gathers the dispersed into unity
to boast, to compete in glory — the lands outshine the sun itself through his radiance
to engulf, to exhaust — the generosity swallows up all desire
to be aimed at, hurled toward — the glances of onlookers thrown toward the poet
to be slow, to delay — the fulfillment of promises long overdue
to be bereaved — to lose what one loves; here the poet is bereaved by the world's disappointments

context: A seven-verse tawil panegyric addressed to a powerful unnamed patron, likely a senior Abbasid official. Abu Tammam uses the sun and cosmic imagery to elevate his patron. The poem blends petition with philosophical reflection on grief and the soul overflowing with sorrow.

titleAr: أبا دلف لم يبق طالب حاجة
titleEn: O Abu Dalaf, No Petitioner Remains but Me

verses.sadr: أبا دلف لم يبق طالب حاجة

verses.ajuz: ولم ير خلق من جداك يخيب ؟ !
لقى حيث لا تهمي علي جنوب

verses.sadr: فإن نلت ما أملت فيك فإنني

translationEn: O Abu Dalaf, no seeker of a need remains among people but me — and the land is parched and barren. Would it please you that I returned from you disappointed, when no created being has ever seen a petitioner go unrewarded by your generosity? And that I made praise into blame, while an orator rose with it among the peoples of the world? How could this be, when you are the possessor of glory and learning from which every people has a share of dew? I have stayed five months in your courtyard, cast down where the south wind does not blow upon me. If I attain what I hoped from you, then I am deserving — and if not, departure is near.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: drought, barrenness — the land and time are parched, making generosity all the more vital
barren, infertile — of land deprived of rain
disappointed, having failed to achieve one's aim
your courtyard, your threshold — the space before the patron's dwelling
the south wind — a warm, rain-bearing wind; its absence heightens the desolation
deserving, worthy — the poet asserts his worthiness of the patron's favor

context: A six-verse tawil petitionary qasida addressed to Abu Dalaf al-'Ijli, one of Abu Tammam's important patrons. The poet reveals he has waited five months in the patron's courtyard — a powerful detail illustrating the desperation of the patronage system. The closing ultimatum — grant me what I seek or I depart — is a bold rhetorical move.

titleEn: Tell the Prince and Find Speech's Battleground

verses.ajuz: أصغى إلى المطل حتى باع ما وهبا !

verses.sadr: إني وإن كان قوم ما لهم سبت
إحفظ وسائل شعر فيك ما ذهبت
verses.ajuz: خواطف البرق إلا دون ما ذهبا

verses.sadr: فلا تضعها فما في الأرض أحسن من
verses.ajuz: نظم القوافي إذا ما صادفت حسبا

verses.sadr: إن أنت لم تك عدل الحق تنصفه
verses.ajuz: لم نرج بعدك خلقا ينص ف الأدبا

translationEn: Tell the prince — you will find speech has its arena there, and you will meet in his shelter both the easy and the spacious. May your sandal be ransomed by a giver endowed with nobility who listened to delays until he sold back what he had given! I — even if there are people who have nothing but the completion of their need, while the connection benefited them ahead of me — harbor a burning pang in my heart, kindled by the fact that I was surpassed and others given the prize. Preserve the means of poetry about you — for the lightning's swift flashes have not gone beyond what they have achieved. They go out as strangers through the lands and never cease to comfort the stranger in the horizons. Do not belittle them — for there is nothing finer on earth than the stringing of rhymes when they meet with noble lineage. If you are not the just champion who gives the right its due, we shall hope after you for no creature who gives letters their proper rank.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a battleground, a place of surging activity — speech finds its arena with the prince
procrastination in paying what is owed — the patron who delayed until he took back his gifts
the prize cane, the winning stake — the first prize in a race
the snatching flashes of lightning — metaphor for the speed and reach of poetry
the stringing of rhymes — the craft of poetry as jewelry-making
lineage, noble ancestry — poetry meets its match in the noble patron

context: An eight-verse basit madih addressed to an unnamed prince. Abu Tammam complains of being bypassed in the patronage queue while affirming the imperishable power of his poetry. The verse on poetry as a stranger that comforts strangers in distant lands is a celebrated statement of poetry's universality.

titleAr: صبرا على المطل مالم يتله الكذب
titleEn: Patience Under Delay When Lies Follow Not

verses.sadr: صبرا على المطل مالم يتله الكذب
على المقادير لوم إن رميت به
ليس الحجاب بمقض عنك لي أملا
مادون بابك لي باب ألوذ به
يا خير من سمعت أذن به ورأت

translationEn: Patience under delay — as long as lies do not follow — for calamities, if you deal gently with them, have an aftermath. Blame upon fate if you are struck by it from a just one — and upon me is the effort and the seeking. O King, whose sighting is distant yet whose generosity is near to those who hope for it! The veil you keep is no barrier to my hope — for the sky is hoped in when it hides itself behind clouds. There is no door before yours to which I might cling, and behind you I have no dwelling place or goal to seek. O best of what an ear has heard and an eye has seen, and to whose doors the Arabs have come. As for silence — it is folded over a ready store — and in your speech the wealth of the gullible is plundered!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: procrastination in payment, delay of what is owed
aftermath, consequence — calamities have an end if treated with patience
the decrees of fate, destinies — from قدر
the veil, the screen — the king's inaccessibility; like clouds before the sky
nearness, proximity — his generosity is always near even if his person is distant
the wealth of the naive, the inexperienced — plundered by one word from the king

context: A seven-verse basit petitionary madih addressed to an unnamed king. Abu Tammam opens with a philosophical maxim on patience, then pivots to petition. The image of the sky hidden behind clouds yet still hoped in is a memorable metaphor for trust in the inaccessible great. The closing verse — silence is rich, but the king's speech plunders wealth — is epigrammatic.

titleEn: None Knows but the Subtle, the Aware

verses.sadr: إن في البشر روضة فإذا كا
فاقسم اللحظ بيننا إن في اللح

translationEn: None knows but God, the Subtle and the Aware, what thing the hearts are folded upon! They say you are the man who defends a friend in his absence and protects him. But when I come visiting you, gloom and ill-temper veil your face from me. So release your cheerfulness with your care — for cheerfulness in most affairs is itself a herald of good. In a smile there is a garden — and when it is paired with giving, it becomes a meadow and a running stream. So divide your glance between us — for in the glance there is the heading of what the inner self conceals!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the Subtle, the Aware — divine epithets from the Quran (67:14, 6:103)
one who gives victory, a strong champion — one who backs his friend powerfully
gloom, depression, downcast expression
frowning, scowling — from بسر, to look sour-faced
cheerfulness of face, a smiling open expression — different from بشر (humankind)
the sideways glance, the look from the corner of the eye

context: A six-verse khafif madih-cum-reproach addressed to a patron who receives visitors with a gloomy face. Abu Tammam appeals for a smile and a cheerful glance, arguing that cheerfulness is itself generosity. The verse on the glance as 'the heading of what the inner self conceals' is a celebrated observation.

titleEn: She Turned Away the Flame of My Besotted Heart

verses.sadr: في كل يوم في فؤادي وقعة
أما الذي في جسمه فسل التي
ما كف من حرب الزمان ورميه
ما إن يزال بجد حزم مقبل

verses.ajuz: ريخ إذا بلغتك إن لم تنحر

verses.sadr: جود كجود السيل إلا أن ذا
جش لي ببخر واحد أغرقك في
كم من كثير البذل قد جازيته

verses.ajuz: من قول باغ أنه لم يثمر

verses.sadr: قد كدت أن أنسى ظماء جوانحي
verses.ajuz: من بعد شقة موردي عن مصدري

translationEn: She turned away the flame of my besotted heart — and I was left the plunder of longing and remembrance. The stars of good fortune set on the day of her departure, and the days have wronged my reception of her. Every day there is a strike in my heart from yearning — except that it is unrecorded. Show me one who was a companion of youth, who kept pace with youth in the arena of sorrows and did not crack! As for what is in his body — ask the one who abandoned him while he was faithful and never abandoned her. A fair-complexioned yellow pallor of illness has settled upon his frame in a garment of yellow sickness. She killed him in secret, then said openly — as al-Farazdaq said — not by a white-bellied gazelle! She looked at him and her glance found no rest until she wished she had never looked. She saw a pallor that troubled her in his body — what troubles you in a thoroughbred horse that is trained lean for racing? He is the target of calamities that keep striking him from an oblique angle with the mother of hardship. Fate has clung to him until it nearly surprises him with what he did not decree for himself. Nothing has stilled the war of time and its blows except his patience — though patience did not grant him victory. He never ceases advancing with firm resolve, treading upon the heels of a livelihood that retreats from him. Know, O life, that a sin will befall you which will weaken you if you reach him without slaughter. How many a barren, desolate expanse has he crossed — then settled in a home through you that is not desolate. By your generosity every wound that festers is healed, the way healers mend with a great skilled remedy. Generosity like the generosity of a flood — except that a flood is turbid, and your giving is clear and pure. The 'Id al-Fitr and the 'Id al-Adha have both passed — yet my hope at your door is still fasting, never having broken fast! A year has passed and your giving has not produced fruit — but a pregnant woman is awaited for nine months! Pour me one hoarse stream and I will drown you in praise from seven hoarse seas. Cut short with your giving the life of your delay — and my gratitude will turn into praise that outlives seven vultures. How many a lavish giver have I recompensed with thanks finer and more abundant than his giving. The worst of first and last is a bond of obligation never honored, and a gift never thanked. Let not my provocations anger you — they are stored up for you in the fullest waterskin. May I ransom you — a leafy promise that did not ransom me from the saying of a transgressor that it bore no fruit. I nearly forgot the thirst in my inner depths after the long distance between my watering place and my starting point. And if you wish, I shall excuse you in full — for incapacity to me is an excuse for the one who is unexcused. I never see myself praising and reproaching you without having set you free — so set me free. And know that today I am planting seeds of praise that will ripen — so harvest them tomorrow in the army camp.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the recklessly besotted one, the one lost in passion without restraint
a thoroughbred horse trained lean for racing — gaunt but powerful, a metaphor for the emaciated lover
the two great Muslim feasts — 'Id al-Fitr (after Ramadan) and 'Id al-Adha (Feast of Sacrifice)
vultures — Luqman's vultures lived for centuries; 'outlives seven vultures' means eternal praise
the fullest waterskin — the stored praise ready to pour forth when needed
a skilled, powerful healer — an archaic expression for a master physician or remedy
the pregnant woman — used as a metaphor for the patron's generosity gestating

context: A major 29-verse kamil qasida that begins as a ghazal nasib about a beloved who has departed, then transitions into a sustained madih addressed to a powerful patron in the poet's military camp (al-'askar). The poem moves through longing, description of the emaciated lover, description of the patron's generosity, and sharp reproach for delay in giving. Abu Tammam uses the extended metaphor of the two Islamic feasts passing without recompense and a pregnant woman awaited for nine months to dramatize his patience.

titleEn: They Laughed at Youth Departing in Regret

verses.sadr: ملك إذا ما الشعر حار ببلدة
إني انتجعتك يا أبا الفضل الذي
عش سالما تبني العلا بيد الندى
واعلم بأني لم أقم بك فاخرا
verses.ajuz: لك مادحا في مدحه لم أنذر

translationEn: They laughed at the regret of receding youth, and wept at the laughter of a moonlit old age. They skirmished against the cavalry of my resolve with a resolve that left in my heart a blow never overcome. They tested my character and found me open-handed in spending a concealed love. They marveled that I gave away my very soul — and so do I marvel at the generosity of Ja'far. A king — when needs cling to his sides, they meet the open palm of his easily flowing giving. A king in whose left hand are the keys of destruction, and whose right is the key to the locked door of the destitute. A king — when poetry loses its way in a city, he becomes the guide to its bewildered gaze. O you who gives me glad tidings of the means of wealth — his very face's bright expression is a harbinger. Take pride in your generosity more than in any other pride — for your gift spreads news about you that you yourself have not spread. I have come seeking pasture from you, O Abu al-Fadl, whose generosity has brought my watering place close to my setting-out point. Live safely, building high glory with the hand of liberality, until you become a rival to the Pleiades. I see the fruit of praise poems ripening, and their branches swaying above their roots. Were it not for you I would never have loosened the reins of my praise poems, and never opened the bolt of my gratitude. Rarely have I drawn up the cavalry of my praise poems without returning with them unvictorious. Was not my homeland in your land, and my longing in Damascus grazing in al-Buhturi's dwelling? I take refuge in your name lest you be like a raincloud that brings no hope, and a sapling that bears no fruit. Know that I did not remain with you boasting for you, praising you with a praise in which I gave no warning.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: moonlit old age — white hair gleaming like moonlight, beautiful yet melancholy
to skirmish, to engage in light combat — women's glances compared to cavalry
a key — from Greek kleis; the patron holds the key to the locked door of the poor
to seek pasture, to travel to a patron in search of sustenance
the Pleiades — the star cluster whose rising signals rain and bounty
a heavy bolt or lock on a door — the lock of gratitude opened by the patron's generosity
al-Buhturi — Abu Tammam's great student and rival, mentioned as inhabiting Damascus

context: A seventeen-verse kamil madih in praise of Ja'far (possibly Ja'far ibn Dinar), opening with a striking nasib in which women laugh at the poet's receding youth. The poem is notable for its direct mention of al-Buhturi, Abu Tammam's famous pupil, as well as the vivid image of the king whose left hand holds the keys of destruction and whose right opens the door of relief for the destitute.

titleEn: Your Pilgrimage Accepted and Your Sin Forgiven

verses.sadr: إلا كتابا لنا قد كنت جدت به
فتب إلى الله من تحقيق باطله
verses.ajuz: فأنت إن تبت عند الله معذور !

translationEn: Now that you have performed the pilgrimage — it is accepted and blessed, your share fulfilled, your sin forgiven. You have fulfilled the obligatory pilgrimage of Islam, then returned — and your effort is thanked. Except for a letter you gave us: its seal was broken, and the content of its text is false. So repent to God for confirming what is false in it — for if you repent, you are excused before God!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a pilgrimage accepted and rewarded by God — the highest grade of hajj
one whose share is complete, whose portion is full
the obligatory pilgrimage of Islam — the once-in-a-lifetime duty of hajj
to break the seal — of a letter; here implying the letter's contents have been exposed
falsehood, fabrication — the content of the letter is revealed to be lies

context: A four-verse basit poem congratulating someone on completing the hajj, then pivoting sharply to reproach: the pilgrim sent a letter containing falsehoods. The ironically pious ending — 'repent to God and you will be excused' — is characteristic of Abu Tammam's wit.

titleEn: O Abu Ali, Time's Turning Is Instructive

verses.sadr: إن أنت لم تترك السير الحثيث إلى
إن النفور له عندي مقر هوى

verses.ajuz: ما فيك من طمحان الأير والنظر

translationEn: O Abu Ali — in time's reversals and its changes, its calamities and days and lessons. You have reminded me of the affair of Dawud — when I was a young man, tossed about in passions and thoughts. If you do not abandon your swift pursuit of the gazelles of Rum, we shall gallop toward the Khazar instead! Do you have the sun before you with her perfected beauties — yet you are preoccupied inwardly with the moon? The elusive one has with me a dwelling of love that takes the place of hearing and sight within me. Many a one more fortified and protected than him in flank has by evening become, through my persistence, in danger. I sent forth against him the armies of resolve — and their darkness retreated from him, to the disgrace of cowardly inaction. Glory be to the One whom every limb glorifies — in you, amid the ambition of lust and of the eye. You are the one who stays put, and his caravans never set out at dusk — while his lust is forever on a journey!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the turns of time, fate's reversals — from صرف, to turn, reverse
changes, vicissitudes — from غيّر, to change
the gazelles of Rum — beautiful Byzantine girls; the beloved is described as a gazelle
the Khazars — a Turkic people north of the Caucasus; a comic hyperbole for seeking beauty elsewhere
the elusive one, the wild one who shies away — the coy beloved
to persist, to keep at it secretly — from وكّت, to mark time upon something

context: A nine-verse basit poem addressed to Abu Ali, mixing reminiscence, erotic teasing, and crude humor. The poem begins as a reflection on time and youth before descending into explicit mockery of the addressee's sexual pursuits. The closing verses employ frank sexual vocabulary characteristic of the more unrestrained poems in Abu Tammam's diwan.

titleAr: ذل السؤال شجى في الحلق معترض
titleEn: The Humiliation of Asking Lodges in the Throat

verses.sadr: ذل السؤال شجى في الحلق معترض
verses.ajuz: من دونه شرق من خلفه جرض

verses.sadr: ما ماء كفك إن جادت وإن بخلت
verses.ajuz: من ماء وجهي إذا أفنيته عوض

verses.sadr: أجر الفراسة من قرني إلى قدمي
verses.ajuz: ومشها حيث لا عثر ولا دحض

verses.sadr: من أشتكي وإلى من أعتزي وندى
verses.ajuz: من أجتدي كل أمري فيك منتقض
لم يأتلوا في ما أعدوا وما ركضوا

verses.sadr: لما فككت رقاب الشعر عن فكري

translationEn: The humiliation of asking is a bone lodged in the throat — before it a choking, behind it a gulp. The water of your palm, whether it flows generously or withholds, is no replacement for the water of my face when I have spent it. I see your affairs: when their paths are trodden they are burning coals, and their rewards are silver coins. With the slightest drawing near from you I expand and open, just as with the slightest rebuff I contract and close. Run the art of physiognomy over me from crown to toe — and walk it where there is no stumble and no slipping. It will inform you that I am no timid shrinker from misfortunes, nor a heavy hulk of decay. To whom shall I complain, to whom shall I boast, and from whom shall I seek the dew of bounty — all my affairs with you are unraveled. A friendship whose fruits have become likened to appearance alone, and a high aim whose substance of beneficence is mere accident. I suspect there are people around you — and I reckon they spared no effort in what they prepared and in how they schemed. They shoot at me with eyes full of sparks, those eyes that speak for hearts full of sickness. Were it not for the clinging of my honor and the waiting for tomorrow and the thirst — fate's perpetual obligation upon me — I would not have loosened the necks of poetry from my thought, nor their necks — except while they are in a state of impurity! I have become: my renown is shot at by his obscurity — and all of him is a target for all of my arrows.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a bone stuck in the throat — the most uncomfortable and shameful impediment
choking on water going down the wrong way — to choke, to be stuck
a gulp at the point of death — the final forced swallow
the water of the face — dignity, self-respect, honor; once spent it cannot be replaced
physiognomy, the art of reading character from physical features
my renown, my distinction — from نبه, to be alert, distinguished, famous

context: A thirteen-verse basit madih-cum-complaint addressed to a patron who has been influenced against the poet by envious rivals. Abu Tammam begins with the celebrated image of the humiliation of petitioning as a bone lodged in the throat, then ranges through self-defense, complaint, and sharp epigrams. The poem is notable for the frank statement that uncompensated praise degrades the poet.

titleEn: A Brother Whom Time's Mixing Has Dictated To

verses.sadr: لو بأسد العريف نيطت عرى المن
وطري في فجاءة الرد ما يع
ضئضئي من بني عدي بن عمر و
verses.ajuz: غير أني في مثلها من ثقيف

verses.sadr: لا تته إن أطال هزك مدحي

translationEn: A brother upon whom time's intermingling has dictated long turning and disposal. Manliness had refined him for me until prolonged beneficence corrupted him. The days made him hate my praise — and so my generous thanks absolved him of his meager giving! Is cutting off noses truly a cutting off — or is it that some of those we choose are themselves the cutting off of noses? If the ropes of obligation were tied to the lions of the den — the necks of the lions of the den would bow in humiliation! My reaction in the suddenness of rejection is what reveals a high ambition and a soul that turns away with dignity. My origin is from Banu 'Adi ibn 'Amr — though in such a situation I find myself among Thaqif. Do not be vain if my praise has long shaken you — and forgive me: after this you are no longer among my swords!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: dictated to him, imposed upon him — as one dictates to a scribe
to refine, purify — the manliness had refined him like a honed blade
the prolongation of beneficence — when generosity is extended too long it breeds contempt
the cutting off of noses — a severe Arabic metaphor for humiliation and dishonor
my origin, my deep root — from ضئضئ, the original stock of a tribe
one who turns away with dignity, who disdains what does not befit him

context: An eight-verse khafif poem addressed to a friend-turned-disappointment — a figure who once had the nobility of manliness but has been corrupted by receiving too much of the poet's praise and too little obligation to reciprocate. The poem moves from lament to sharp epigram to a final dismissal: 'you are no longer among my swords' — you are expelled from the arsenal of those I will praise.
